original_abstract: The distribution of ions at the air/water interface is a fundamental driver of numerous chemical and environmental processes. For years, the prevailing scientific consensus has suggested that large, polarizable ions are surface-active, congregating at the very top of the liquid phase to generate electric fields that dictate interfacial water organization. In this study, we challenge this conventional model using a combination of heterodyne-detected vibrational sum-frequency generation and neural network-assisted ab initio molecular dynamics simulations. Our findings reveal that ions in common electrolyte solutions do not reside at the immediate surface, but are instead situated in a distinct subsurface region. This creates a stratified interface characterized by a pristine, ion-depleted outermost layer and an ion-enriched layer underneath. This subsurface stratification provides a new conceptual framework for understanding how ions reorganize water molecules at the boundary between air and water.
humanized_abstract: The distribution of ions at the air/water interface is highly relevant to numerous chemical and environmental phenomena. Long-standing wisdom in the scientific community is that large polarizable ions are surface active and that these ions accumulate at the air/water surface headspace to generate interfacial electric fields that influence structure and arrangement of interfacial water molecules. Here, we have challenged this prevailing wisdom using heterodyne-detected vibrational sum-frequency generation (VSFG) and neural network-assisted ab initio molecular dynamics simulations. The results show that the distribution of ions in common electrolyte solutions is not as previously assumed, but that ions are situated at some subsurface depth. This generates a stratified interfacial structure with a very clean and pure ion depleted air/water interface headspace and an ion rich subsurface layer, thereby introducing new insights into ion-induced structuring of the air/water interface.

The results show that the distribution of ions in common electrolyte solutions is not as previously assumed, but that ions are situated at some subsurface depth. This generates a stratified interfacial structure with a very clean and pure ion depleted air/water interface headspace and an ion rich subsurface layer, thereby introducing new insights into ion-induced structuring of the air/water interface.
